sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture departments, the Australian apple and pear industry, and horticultural research.

core.intro: Wherever you are in Australia, the basics of growing apples are the same: a sunny, open spot, free-draining soil, a variety matched to your local winter chill, and usually a second variety nearby to pollinate it. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you live; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: The first choice, and the one most likely to make or break your tree, is matching the variety to your winter chill. Mainstream apples such as Gala, Fuji, Jonathan, Granny Smith and the Red and Golden Delicious types need a properly cold winter (around 600 hours below about 7 degrees) to flower and fruit well, so they are reliable only in genuinely cold districts. For warm, coastal and subtropical gardens, choose low-chill varieties such as Anna, Dorsett Golden and Tropic Sweet, which fruit on only a few hundred chill hours. The West Australian-bred Cripps Pink (sold as Pink Lady), Cripps Red (sold as Sundowner) and their parent Lady Williams sit in their own group: they take only modest winter chill but ripen very late and need a long, warm to hot growing season, so they suit warm-temperate and Mediterranean districts rather than cold, short-season ones. Work out what your climate can ripen first, then pick a named variety to match.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: Most apples are self-incompatible, meaning a tree sets little or no fruit with its own pollen and crops properly only when a different variety flowers nearby at the same time, within bee range. A few, including Granny Smith, have a high degree of self-fertility and will set some fruit alone, but even these crop more heavily with a partner, while varieties like Pink Lady simply need cross-pollination. Pick two varieties whose flowering overlaps (most mid-season apples overlap happily). Watch out for triploid varieties such as Gravenstein, Jonagold and Mutsu (Crispin): their pollen is effectively sterile, so they cannot pollinate other trees and in fact need two other varieties to pollinate them. If you have room for only one tree, plant a self-fertile variety, a multi-graft tree that carries several varieties on one trunk, or a flowering crab-apple as a near-universal pollinator.

sections.heading: Rootstocks and tree size
sections.body: Apples are grafted onto a rootstock that sets the tree's eventual size, and this choice matters more for apples than for almost any other fruit tree. Fully dwarfing stocks such as M9 hold a tree to about two or three metres and bring it into bearing in two to three years, but need a permanent stake. M26 is a slightly larger semi-dwarf. MM106 is the popular backyard stock, semi-vigorous, quick to crop, resistant to woolly apple aphid and able to stand without permanent staking. Vigorous stocks such as MM111 or a seedling rootstock make large, long-lived trees that can take four or five years to fruit and suit only big gardens or orchards. For a courtyard, a balcony, a pot or an espalier, choose a dwarfing rootstock or a naturally compact columnar type such as the Ballerina apples.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Apples want a sunny, open position (full sun for most of the day) and a deep, free-draining soil with a pH of about 6 to 7. They dislike waterlogging, so avoid any spot where water sits, and plant on a low mound if your soil is heavy. Space trees to suit the rootstock, from a couple of metres for dwarf trees up to four to six metres for vigorous ones. Apples are deciduous and are usually sold and planted bare-rooted in winter while dormant, though potted trees can go in through much of the year as long as you keep the water up over their first summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Young trees need regular water to establish, and any bearing tree needs steady, even moisture while the fruit is filling. Inconsistent watering is a common cause of bitter pit, the small brown spots in the flesh, which is linked to too little calcium reaching the fruit and is made worse when dry spells slow the movement of calcium into the apple, so water evenly and add calcium as dolomite if your soil is low in it. Aim for roughly 25mm of water a week through the growing season from rain or irrigation. Apples are light to moderate feeders and nitrogen is usually the main nutrient to add each year, as a complete fertiliser or as compost. Go easy: a useful guide is only about 5 to 18 grams of actual nitrogen per tree for each year of the tree's age, up to roughly 140 grams a year, because too much nitrogen drives soft leafy growth at the expense of fruit, gives poorly coloured apples and hurts storage life. Feed in late winter to early spring before growth starts and again after harvest if needed, but not late in the season, which delays the tree hardening off for winter.

sections.heading: Harvest and storage
sections.body: Apples flower in spring and ripen from summer into autumn, so the Australian harvest runs from about February for early varieties like Gala through to May, and into June for the very late Cripps Pink, Cripps Red and Lady Williams. An apple is ready when its green background colour warms towards yellow, the pips inside turn brown, and a ripe fruit lifts off easily when you cup it and twist upward rather than tugging it down; keen growers confirm timing with a starch-iodine test, since starch turns to sugar as the fruit matures. Apples are climacteric, so they keep ripening after picking and store well: hold sound, unbruised fruit somewhere cold and dark (close to 0 degrees is ideal, the crisper drawer works at home), and firm late keepers such as Granny Smith and Pink Lady will last for weeks to months.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than an unnamed seedling, because apples do not come true from seed and a seedling can take many years to fruit, if it ever fruits well. Check the label for the variety, the rootstock and the pollination needs, and plan a pollination partner from the start: a second compatible variety, a multi-graft tree, or a flowering crab-apple. Apples and their pome-fruit cousin the pear share much the same care and pests, so they make natural companions in a garden. The in-stock table above lists the named varieties, sizes and prices available right now, including low-chill types for warm districts and dwarf and columnar trees for pots and small gardens.

faqs.q: Can I grow an apple tree in a pot or on a balcony?
faqs.a: Yes, if you start with a tree on a dwarfing rootstock or a slim columnar variety. Use a large container of free-draining potting mix, water often because pots dry out fast (and uneven watering spoils the fruit), and feed regularly through the growing season since nutrients wash out of containers. Refresh the top of the mix or pot up every couple of years, and stand the pot in the sunniest spot you have.

faqs.q: Do apple trees lose their leaves in winter?
faqs.a: Yes. Apples are deciduous, dropping their leaves in autumn and resting through winter, and it is that cold rest that lets them flower in spring, which is why they need a cold-enough winter to crop. Bare-rooted trees are sold and planted in this dormant season, which is the cheapest and often the best way to buy one.

faqs.q: Why is my apple tree healthy but not fruiting?
faqs.a: The usual reasons are that the tree is still too young (many take three to four years to settle into bearing), that there is no compatible variety flowering nearby to pollinate it, or that it is taking an off year after a heavy crop. A variety that needs more winter chill than your district provides, or a tree fed too much nitrogen, will also grow leaves instead of fruit, so check the variety suits your climate and feed sparingly.

faqs.q: Should I thin the fruit on my apple tree?
faqs.a: Usually yes. Apples often set far more fruit than they can size up, so thinning each cluster to one or two of the best fruit in early summer, after the natural fruit drop, gives larger, better apples and stops branches breaking. Thinning also helps prevent biennial bearing, the habit of cropping heavily one year and barely at all the next.

WA.intro: Growing apples in Western Australia. WA is a serious apple state with a remarkable distinction: it is one of the last apple-growing regions on earth still free of codling moth, the pest that troubles orchards almost everywhere else. It is also the birthplace of Pink Lady.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: How well apples grow in WA depends on where you are. The Perth Hills and the cooler Southern Forests around Manjimup and Pemberton get enough winter chill for mainstream varieties, while the warm coastal plain is lower in chill and suits low-chill types better. WA's hot, dry summers are an advantage for apples: they ripen the late varieties fully and keep the fungal diseases that plague the humid eastern states in check.

sections.heading: Where apples grow in WA
sections.body: Western Australia is one of the country's six apple states, and most of its apples come from the cool South West. The Manjimup and Pemberton districts, marketed as the Southern Forests, grow about two thirds of the state's apples, with Donnybrook and the Perth Hills making up most of the rest at roughly 15 per cent each. The Department's horticultural research institute at Manjimup has long bred and trialled temperate fruit, including apples, for these conditions.

sections.heading: The Pink Lady story: home-grown apples
sections.body: Some of the world's best-known apples were bred right here. In the early 1970s John Cripps, working for the Western Australian Department of Agriculture, crossed Golden Delicious with the firm, long-keeping local variety Lady Williams to create Cripps Pink, sold around the world as Pink Lady, and its sibling Cripps Red, sold as Sundowner. All three take only modest winter chill but ripen very late, rewarding WA's long, warm summers, which is exactly why they were bred here. If you garden in the south-west, you can grow these home-grown varieties on their home ground.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's warm summers bring the crop on from about February, starting with early varieties like Gala. The late local varieties hold on much longer: Cripps Pink (Pink Lady) and Cripps Red (Sundowner) are picked through May, with Lady Williams the last apple of all, often into June.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Western Australia has a rare advantage: it is one of the last apple-growing regions on earth still free of codling moth, the grub that is the worst apple pest almost everywhere else, so WA gardeners are spared the spraying their eastern counterparts need. The fruit fly to watch here is instead the Mediterranean fruit fly, which is endemic in WA and will attack apples. Australia as a whole is also free of fire blight, a serious apple and pear disease, and keeping it out is a national biosecurity priority.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine is part of why it stays free of codling moth and other pests, so apple trees and budwood from interstate must be listed as permitted on the WA Organism List and are usually treated and certified before they can enter. Many eastern nurseries will not post live plants to WA as a result, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here, including WA growers of the home-bred varieties.

faqs.q: Which apple varieties suit Perth and the south-west?
faqs.a: In the Perth Hills and the cooler Southern Forests, the home-grown Cripps Pink (Pink Lady), Cripps Red (Sundowner) and Lady Williams are natural picks, alongside Gala, Fuji and Granny Smith. On the warmer, lower-chill coastal plain, lean on low-chill types such as Anna and Dorsett Golden, which fruit without a hard winter.

faqs.q: Can apple trees cope with Perth's sandy coastal soils?
faqs.a: They can, but sandy soil needs help. Dig in plenty of compost before planting, mulch thickly, use a soil wetting agent if the sand has turned water-repellent, and feed little and often because nutrients leach quickly through sand. Keeping the water even also matters, since sandy soils dry out fast and erratic watering spoils the fruit. If you can, plant in the cooler hills for better chill.

QLD.intro: Growing apples in Queensland. Apples want a cold winter, so in Queensland they are a high-country crop: the Granite Belt in the far south is cold enough for a full commercial industry, while the warm coast and subtropics can only manage low-chill varieties.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Most of Queensland is too warm in winter to chill apples properly, so they are a crop for the cool, elevated south. The standout exception is the Granite Belt, high enough to deliver real winter cold. Away from there, in the subtropical and coastal districts including Brisbane, only low-chill varieties will fruit, and even then the crops are lighter and less reliable than further south.

sections.heading: Where apples grow in QLD
sections.body: Practically all of Queensland's apples are grown on the Granite Belt around Stanthorpe, Applethorpe and Ballandean, near the New South Wales border. Sitting at 800 to 1000 metres, the Granite Belt has the highest winter chill in the state, with sub-zero nights and spring frosts, which is exactly what apples need to crop well.

sections.heading: Low-chill apples for the subtropics
sections.body: In subtropical south-east Queensland the low-chill varieties are the ones to plant: Anna, Dorsett Golden and Tropic Sweet fruit on only a few hundred chill hours and are sold for exactly these warm conditions. Give them the sunniest, most open position you have, plant two for cross-pollination, and expect a lighter crop than the same trees would set in a cold district.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: On the Granite Belt the apple harvest opens around February, with the bulk of the fruit picked through March and April and the last of the late varieties into May. The Stanthorpe district celebrates it with an apple and grape harvest festival.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Unlike Western Australia, Queensland has codling moth, the key apple pest, so growers in the cold districts manage it actively. Queensland fruit fly is endemic across the state and readily attacks apples, so backyard fruit usually needs exclusion bags, netting or a baiting program. In wet, humid seasons apple scab, also called black spot, can mark both leaves and fruit.

faqs.q: Can you grow apples in Brisbane or coastal Queensland?
faqs.a: You can, but only the low-chill varieties such as Anna, Dorsett Golden and Tropic Sweet will fruit in Brisbane's mild winters, and even they give lighter, less certain crops than the same trees in a cold district. Choose the warmest, sunniest spot, plant two for pollination, and protect the fruit from fruit fly. For dependable apples, the Granite Belt is the place to head.

faqs.q: Which apple varieties suit the Granite Belt?
faqs.a: Because the Granite Belt has a genuinely cold winter, it grows the same mainstream apples as the southern states: Gala, Fuji, Jonathan, Red and Golden Delicious, Granny Smith and Pink Lady all crop well there. That cold winter is the whole reason Queensland's commercial apples sit high in the south rather than near the coast.

NSW.intro: Growing apples in New South Wales. NSW grows apples in its cool tablelands and mountain districts rather than on the warm, humid coast, and it holds a special place in apple history as the birthplace of the Granny Smith.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: New South Wales grows apples wherever winters are cold enough, which means the cool tablelands and mountain districts rather than the warm coast and plains. The high country supplies the winter chill apples need, while the coast suits only low-chill varieties and carries more fungal disease pressure through humid springs. Choose your variety to match your district.

sections.heading: Where apples grow in NSW
sections.body: The main apple districts are all cool and elevated. Batlow, high on the western slopes of the Snowy Mountains, is the most famous and supplies around a tenth of the national crop. Orange, on the Central Tablelands below Mount Canobolas, is the other big commercial district, and Bilpin, up in the Blue Mountains on the Bells Line of Road, is long known as the land of the mountain apple.

sections.heading: The home of the Granny Smith
sections.body: New South Wales gave the world the Granny Smith. In 1868, at Eastwood in the Ryde district of Sydney, Maria Ann Smith examined a chance apple seedling growing where she had tipped out the remains of some Tasmanian crab apples. It proved to be an outstanding green apple, good for cooking and eating and a superb keeper, and it now grows around the world. It is a reminder that every famous variety began as a single lucky seedling.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: The New South Wales harvest runs from about February through to May. Early varieties come in first, and the coolest, highest districts such as Batlow and Orange finish later than the warmer areas, which spreads the season out.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: New South Wales has codling moth, the main apple pest, across its growing areas. Queensland fruit fly is established along the coast and inland and uses apples as a host, so backyard fruit in warmer districts often needs protecting. Apple scab, or black spot, is the chief fungal worry in wet springs, especially in the humid coastal and mountain districts.

faqs.q: Which apple varieties suit Sydney and the warm NSW coast?
faqs.a: Sydney's mild, humid winters suit low-chill varieties best, so Anna, Dorsett Golden and Tropic Sweet are the safe picks for a coastal backyard, with lighter crops than you would get inland. If you garden up in the cool tablelands or mountains, you can grow the full range of mainstream apples instead.

faqs.q: How do I stop fruit fly ruining my apples?
faqs.a: Bag or sleeve the developing fruit, net the whole tree, or run a trapping and baiting program, and always pick up and bin fallen fruit promptly so the maggots cannot complete their cycle. Fruit fly control works far better when a whole neighbourhood does it together rather than one garden acting alone.

VIC.intro: Growing apples in Victoria. Victoria grows more apples than any other state: its cool winters give apples the chill they need right across the state, and its orchards pioneered some of the growing systems used worldwide.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's cool winters supply plenty of the chill apples need, almost everywhere in the state, which is a big reason it grows more apples than anywhere else in Australia. Warm, dry inland summers ripen the fruit well, while the cooler, wetter districts near the coast and ranges carry more disease pressure and ripen a little later in the season.

sections.heading: Where apples grow in VIC
sections.body: Victoria grows about 43 per cent of Australia's apples, more than any other state. The heartland is the Goulburn Valley around Shepparton, Ardmona and Cobram, long the centre of the pome-fruit and fruit-canning industry. The cooler, wetter Yarra Valley near Melbourne, the Harcourt district near Bendigo, and the high country around Stanley and Beechworth are the other main regions.

sections.heading: Intensive orchards and the Tatura Trellis
sections.body: Victoria is the home of the Tatura Trellis, a high-density, V-shaped orchard system worked out at the Tatura research station in the Goulburn Valley from the 1970s to crop more fruit, sooner, from a given area. Home gardeners borrow the same idea on a small scale by training an apple flat as an espalier or along wires, which suits a courtyard or a sunny fence and makes a heavy crop easy to reach.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's harvest runs from about February into May, and a little later again in the cool high country. That spread of districts and altitudes is part of why the state can supply apples over such a long season.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Codling moth is present in Victoria and is the main apple pest to manage. Apple scab, or black spot, is the key disease and is worst in the wetter districts such as the Yarra Valley. Queensland fruit fly has become established in parts of the state, including the Goulburn Murray Valley, and attacks apples, so fruit fly control is now part of growing apples across many areas.

faqs.q: Which apple varieties suit Melbourne and southern Victoria?
faqs.a: Almost any apple grows around Melbourne, since the winter chill is ample. In the cooler, damper southern districts, lean towards scab-tolerant varieties and keep the canopy open to the air, because black spot is the main challenge there. The firm late keepers like Granny Smith and Pink Lady do especially well through the long, cool autumn.

faqs.q: Can I espalier an apple tree against a fence in a small garden?
faqs.a: Yes, and Victoria's cool climate is ideal for it. Plant a tree on a dwarfing rootstock, fan or step its branches flat against a sunny wall or along wires, and prune in summer to keep it neat. An espaliered apple takes very little space, crops well for its size, and makes both picking and netting easy.

further_reading.title: The Granny Smith Apple and the Tatura Trellis (Bas van den Ende)
